heroHeading: Modular Home Extensions, Built for Manchester
metaTitle: Modular Home Extensions in Manchester | EazyBase
metaDescription: Factory-built modular extensions for Manchester homes — designed for red-brick terraces and semis, built in Blackburn, installed in under a week.
intro:
Manchester's homes were built to last — but they weren't always built for the way we live now. Whether you're in a red-brick terrace in Levenshulme, a bay-fronted semi in Chorlton or a Victorian villa in Didsbury, the story is often the same: you love the house and the street, you just need more of it. That's exactly what EazyBase was set up to solve.

We design and deliver modular home extensions in Manchester — kitchens, dining spaces, home offices, playrooms and garden rooms — built in our Blackburn factory in as little as four weeks, then installed at your home in under a week. Because the build happens off-site, you skip months of skips, scaffolding and mud, and keep the disruption to days rather than seasons.

And with our factory just up the road, Manchester isn't at the edge of our patch — it's the heart of it. From first sketch to final fix, you're dealing with one local team who know these streets and these houses.

localAngles.heading: Extending a Manchester terrace
localAngles.body: The classic Manchester terrace is generous in character but tight in footprint — a narrow kitchen at the back, a modest yard or garden beyond it. A well-designed rear extension can transform that layout into a proper kitchen-diner or family room without losing the period charm out front. Because our modules are precision-built in the factory, we design them to sit neatly against existing brickwork and work within the tighter dimensions terraces demand. We match the new to the old where it matters, and make the most of every square metre where it counts.

localAngles.heading: Garden offices for hybrid working
localAngles.body: Manchester has one of the UK's liveliest hybrid workforces, and the spare bedroom desk only works for so long. A garden office gives you a proper commute of thirty seconds — a warm, insulated, fully wired room that's separate from the noise of family life. Because garden rooms are often smaller, self-contained structures, they can frequently be installed with even less disruption than a house extension, and in many gardens they fall within permitted development. For a lot of our Manchester customers, it's the single most life-improving room they add.

localAngles.heading: Planning, permitted development and conservation areas
localAngles.body: Much of Greater Manchester's housing benefits from permitted development rights, which often cover single-storey rear extensions and many garden rooms — though limits on size, height and position apply, and it always pays to check with Manchester City Council (or Trafford, Salford or Stockport, depending on where you live) before work begins. Parts of Didsbury, Chorlton and other neighbourhoods sit within conservation areas, where the rules are tighter and design quality matters more. We're used to both routes: we'll help you understand what applies to your address and design something that fits.

localAngles.heading: Extend or move? The Manchester maths
localAngles.body: Moving up a rung in south Manchester's popular suburbs means stamp duty, agent and legal fees, removal costs — and often a bidding war for a house that still needs work. Extending lets you put that money into the home you already own, on the street you already love, near the schools and tram stops you already rely on. With most EazyBase projects landing between £20,000 and £60,000, an extra room often costs a fraction of the true cost of moving — and you never have to pack a single box.

faqs.q: How quickly can you build and install an extension in Manchester?
faqs.a: Faster than you might think. Your extension is built in our Blackburn factory — around half an hour up the M66 — in as little as four weeks, while your home stays completely untouched. Installation on-site then typically takes under a week. Compare that with a traditional build, where trades can be in your garden for three to six months.

faqs.q: Will I need planning permission in Manchester?
faqs.a: Not always. Permitted development rights often cover single-storey rear extensions and many garden rooms, subject to size and position limits — but you should always check with your local authority, whether that's Manchester City Council, Trafford, Salford or Stockport. If your home sits in a conservation area, as parts of Didsbury and Chorlton do, extra rules apply. We'll help you confirm the right route before any work starts.

faqs.q: My street is a tight terraced road — can you still deliver a modular extension?
faqs.a: In most cases, yes. Access is one of the first things we assess, usually at your free site survey. Manchester's terraced streets, alleyways and rear entries are familiar territory for our installation team, and we plan the delivery, lifting and positioning around your specific plot before anything is built. If there's a constraint, we design for it — not around you after the fact.

faqs.q: What do Manchester homeowners typically use their extensions for?
faqs.a: Open-plan kitchen-diners are the most popular choice for terraces and semis, closely followed by garden offices for hybrid workers. Families often add playrooms or snugs, and garden rooms double as gyms, studios and teenage retreats. Every EazyBase extension is designed around how you actually want to live, so the same footprint can serve very different lives.
